summary: DHH argues that sabbaticals are a simple but powerful tool for employee retention in tech, where the average tenure is just 18 months because workers quit to get extended breaks. 37signals has offered six-week sabbaticals every three years for fifteen years, which he credits with keeping attrition low. He positions this as a way to combine American-level tech pay with European-style time off. He also argues sabbaticals benefit founders by curing burnout that might otherwise lead them to sell their companies prematurely.
key_points: The 18-month average tenure in tech is partly driven by workers quitting just to get a long break, which sabbaticals could prevent
A six-week break allows a mental reset that two-week vacations cannot achieve — you need enough time to actually get bored
The retention argument is purely economic: replacing smart, trained people is expensive
Sabbaticals cure founder burnout that leads to premature company sales — by week five, most founders are antsy to return
American tech workers can have both high pay and regular resets without adopting the European model of lower compensation
money_quotes: The only way many tech workers in the US can get a long break is by quitting their job.
It's been magical for retention because a break like that allows the mind to reset in a way a two-week vacation never could.
I think it's entirely possible to have it both ways: Work for an American tech company with American pay levels, but also enjoy a regular full reset, without having to quit to get it.
It's very expensive to see smart, trained people walk out the door.
Six weeks is also just long enough to remind tired founders that selling their company isn't likely to be the bliss they imagine.
There are endless stories of founders who regret selling their business when all they needed was a six-week break from the startup sprint.
Not just two weeks on Mallorca, but time enough to get bored.
key_insight: Sabbaticals are a cheap retention tool that prevents both employees from quitting and founders from selling — most people don't want to escape work permanently, they just need enough time off to get bored and hungry for it again.
